evidence: QueryExecutor interface has 6 methods with a single concrete implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor); the interface provides genuine testability value but the 'one-impl interface' pattern is present (scan: one_impl_interface_count=1)

BlackLabSnippetParser contains alias methods that add no behavior: extractLemmaAt() delegates to extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition(); extractHeadLemma() delegates to extractLemmaAt(xml,1); extractCollocateLemma() repeats the last-lemma loop already in extractLemmaFromMatch()

Both ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers build HTTP responses using raw Map<String,Object> with repeated manual .put() calls — no typed response DTO, no serialization helper abstraction — wide 'options bag' output pattern present across all handlers (scan: wide_param_bag_count=4)
escapeForRegex() is a stand-alone utility duplicated independently in PatternSubstitution (api) and GrammarConfigLoader.RelationConfig (config), indicating a missing shared abstraction

SemanticFieldExplorer result inner classes (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, etc.) are simple public fields with no encapsulation logic — they are plain data bags nested inside an orchestrator class rather than a proper model abstraction

GrammarConfigLoader.RelationConfig is a rich, cohesive inner record-like class with multiple well-focused methods (getFullPattern, collocatePosGroup, headPosition, collocatePosition, getDeprel) — this abstraction pays for itself
LogDiceCalculator is a focused single-formula utility with two overloads (double/long) that each add clear value — good abstraction
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens is a genuine shared primitive used by both PatternSubstitution and GrammarConfigLoader — well-justified extraction
SemanticFieldExplorer.ExploreOptions record bundles 7 parameters into a named type, eliminating a 10-argument method call — good use of parameter object pattern
Scan evidence shows delegation_heavy_class_count=0 and facade_module_count=0 — no mechanical delegation or re-export issues

strengths: GrammarConfigLoader.RelationConfig is a well-justified abstraction that encapsulates pattern manipulation, positional derivation, and POS group inference in one cohesive type
ExploreOptions record eliminates a 10-parameter method signature and gives the parameters a named, navigable shape
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens is correctly extracted as a shared primitive serving two consuming packages without duplication
LogDiceCalculator provides a focused, mathematically clear formula with proper edge-case handling
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The dominant abstraction problem is that HTTP response construction relies on raw Map<String,Object> bags across all handlers, while simultaneously some utility methods (alias methods in BlackLabSnippetParser, escapeForRegex copies) add indirection without adding value.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase's good abstractions (RelationConfig, ExploreOptions, CqlUtils) reflect intentional design, but the score is pulled down to the upper-50s by the systematic absence of typed response objects (every handler builds raw maps), the alias-method indirection in BlackLabSnippetParser that adds navigation cost without behavior, and the duplicated utility method across packages. Compared to an average codebase (~50), this is slightly above average in abstraction quality for domain logic but notably below on output/serialization discipline.

issues.summary: All HTTP handlers build responses via raw Map<String,Object> with manual .put() calls instead of typed response objects
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore: Map<String,Object> response = new HashMap<>(); response.put("status", "ok"); response.put("seed", ...) — approximately 25 manual put calls
handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti repeats the same pattern with ~20 put calls
handleSemanticField in ExplorationHandlers has ~15 put calls with nested list building
All three handlers in SketchHandlers follow the same raw-map pattern
issues.suggestion: Define typed response records (e.g. SemanticFieldExploreResponse, MultiSeedExploreResponse) and annotate them with fastjson2 @JSONField if needed. This collapses the manual put calls, makes the response contract visible in the type system, and eliminates the risk of mismatched key strings across callers.

issues.summary: BlackLabSnippetParser contains alias methods that add indirection without adding behavior
evidence: extractLemmaAt(xml, position) is a one-line wrapper for extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition(xml, position) with only a renamed method and a new Javadoc
extractHeadLemma(matchXml) is a one-liner: return extractLemmaAt(matchXml, 1)
extractCollocateLemma(matchXml) duplicates the last-lemma scan loop already in extractLemmaFromMatch
issues.suggestion: Consolidate to a single positional extraction method (extractLemmaAt) and remove or inline the aliases. The 'head' and 'collocate' concepts should be handled by the callers using explicit position arguments rather than by adding named wrappers.

issues.summary: escapeForRegex utility duplicated in two packages with no shared abstraction
evidence: PatternSubstitution.escapeForRegex: public static method escaping backslash and double-quote
GrammarConfigLoader.RelationConfig.escapeForRegex: private static method with identical body
issues.suggestion: Add escapeForRegex as a package-accessible static method in CqlUtils (which already provides the shared CQL parsing primitive), and remove both duplicates.

root_causes: Response serialization never received a dedicated abstraction — each handler was written to return raw maps, and the pattern solidified
BlackLabSnippetParser grew incrementally, with new named methods added for convenience rather than behavioral distinction
likely_symptoms: raw_map_response_building is a systemic pattern, not an isolated case
possible_false_positives: QueryExecutor single-impl interface — this is justified by testability needs and BlackLab API isolation, not a false abstraction
